Name: spectral_curvature_routing_quantization
Title: Spectral Curvature-Aware Routing: Training-Free Precision Allocation via Deterministic Hessian Conditioning Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: Layer-wise precision allocation in mixed-precision quantization can be formalized through deterministic, training-free spectral estimation of local Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance structure, enabling a routing protocol that couples geometric subspace diagnostics with provable stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds. This approach replaces heuristic precision assignment with mathematically transparent, curvature-aware routing that yields superior accuracy-efficiency Pareto fronts while maintaining strict O(n) computational overhead and single-GPU feasibility.
Related Work: Existing training-free quantization proxies (EMQ, SNIP) and adaptive mixed-precision frameworks (AMP-ViT) employ layer-wise bit-width search based on activation statistics, gradient magnitudes, or learned heuristics, but lack formal geometric justification for precision decisions. Prior work on Hessian-aware quantization (e.g., second-order sensitivity) requires expensive Hessian-vector products or training-time overhead. Our proposal distinctly differs by: (1) introducing deterministic, pre-execution spectral estimators of local curvature (via power iteration on weight covariance and gradient Jacobian blocks) requiring zero training iterations, (2) formalizing precision routing through explicit condition number thresholds and stability radii derived from PAC-Bayes bounds rather than heuristic thresholds, (3) enforcing coarse-grained block-level routing (layer or attention-head clusters) to guarantee O(n) overhead and deterministic execution, and (4) systematically isolating algorithmic pathologies (gradient covariance decay, feature entanglement) from infrastructural noise through projection-based mechanistic probes.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization balances model accuracy and computational efficiency, yet existing methods rely on heuristic proxies or training-based search lacking formal geometric justification. We propose Spectral Curvature-Aware Routing (SCAR), a training-free framework that allocates layer-wise precision through deterministic spectral estimation of local Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance structure. SCAR couples three core components: (1) Pre-execution Spectral Diagnostics—computing condition numbers and spectral decay rates via power iteration on weight covariance and gradient Jacobian blocks (O(n) overhead), (2) Curvature-Aware Routing Logic—assigning precision tiers to blocks based on explicit stability radii derived from PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and local Lipschitz constants, and (3) Deterministic Precision Switching—enforcing coarse-grained block-level tiering (e.g., layer or multi-head clusters) to preserve architectural coherence and eliminate per-element routing fragility. We validate SCAR on ResNets, Vision Transformers, and LLM inference through systematic ablation of routing mechanisms, quantifying amortized diagnostic overhead, and comparing accuracy-efficiency Pareto fronts against EMQ, SNIP, and manual baselines. Theoretical contributions include formal PAC-Bayes verification that curvature-aware precision allocation satisfies bounded perturbation models under quantization, and explicit numerical stability analysis ensuring no silent precision-switching failures. Empirical validation demonstrates that SCAR achieves 1–3% higher accuracy at equivalent bit budgets while maintaining strict computational overhead bounds, establishing a principled, mathematically transparent alternative to heuristic mixed-precision strategies.

Experiments.name: Spectral Diagnostic Validation
Experiments.description: For ResNet-50 and ViT-B/32, compute pre-execution spectral diagnostics: (1) condition number κ(W^T W) for each layer via power iteration (20 iterations, single-GPU), (2) gradient Jacobian spectral decay via randomized SVD on mini-batch gradients (64 samples), (3) feature entanglement measured as normalized rank of feature covariance matrix. Correlate these diagnostics against post-quantization accuracy drops under uniform 8-bit and 4-bit quantization. Metric: Spearman rank correlation between condition number and accuracy retention (target: ρ > 0.75).

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Bound Calibration
Experiments.description: Derive layer-specific stability radii from PAC-Bayes bounds: for each layer, compute empirical Rademacher complexity via spectral estimators, then compute the margin-dependent PAC-Bayes bound M(δ) = (1/n) Σ loss + O(√(log(1/δ)/n)). Map condition numbers to precision tiers: κ < 10 → 4-bit, 10 ≤ κ < 50 → 6-bit, κ ≥ 50 → 8-bit. Validate bound tightness by comparing predicted stability radius against empirical perturbation tolerance under quantization noise (add Gaussian noise with variance matching quantization error, measure accuracy retention). Metric: Empirical accuracy retention vs. bound-predicted retention (target: <5% gap).

Experiments.name: Curvature-Aware Routing Protocol
Experiments.description: Implement SCAR routing logic: (1) Pre-compute condition numbers and stability radii for all layers (5 min on single GPU for ResNet-50), (2) assign precision tiers via threshold-based routing: if κ(layer) > threshold_high, use 8-bit; else if κ > threshold_mid, use 6-bit; else 4-bit. Thresholds derived from PAC-Bayes bounds. (3) Enforce block-level routing: group layers into 4–8 precision clusters to maintain O(n) overhead. Compare against: (a) uniform quantization (all 4-bit, 6-bit, 8-bit), (b) EMQ heuristic, (c) per-layer random routing. Metrics: (i) Accuracy at fixed bit budget (e.g., 5.5 avg bits), (ii) amortized diagnostic overhead (diagnostic time / inference time), (iii) memory footprint of routing tables.

Experiments.name: Gradient Covariance Decay Analysis
Experiments.description: Isolate algorithmic pathologies: for each layer, compute gradient covariance matrix G = E[∇L ∇L^T] via mini-batch sampling (256 samples). Measure spectral decay: compute eigenvalue spectrum λ_1 ≥ λ_2 ≥ ... and report effective rank (# eigenvalues > 0.01 λ_max). Correlate effective rank against quantization sensitivity: apply layer-wise quantization at varying bit-widths and measure accuracy drops. Hypothesis: layers with slow spectral decay (high effective rank) require higher precision. Metric: Correlation between effective rank and bit-width requirement (target: ρ > 0.70). Ablation: compare against simpler proxies (gradient norm, activation range).

Experiments.name: Feature Entanglement Diagnostics
Experiments.description: For Vision Transformers, measure feature entanglement in attention heads via covariance rank. For each head, compute activation covariance Cov(A) where A is the attention output. Measure normalized rank: r_norm = rank(Cov(A)) / min(d, N). Hypothesis: high-entanglement heads (r_norm > 0.8) require higher precision; low-entanglement heads (r_norm < 0.3) tolerate 4-bit. Assign precision based on r_norm thresholds. Compare against uniform head-wise quantization. Metrics: (i) Accuracy at fixed bit budget, (ii) per-head precision variance (should be non-trivial to justify fine-grained routing).

Experiments.name: Comprehensive Accuracy-Efficiency Pareto Front
Experiments.description: Evaluate SCAR on three architectures: (i) ResNet-50 on ImageNet (top-1 accuracy), (ii) ViT-B/32 on ImageNet, (iii) OPT-6.7B on WikiText-103 (perplexity). For each, sweep bit budgets (4–8 avg bits per layer) and report: (a) accuracy/perplexity, (b) model size (MB), (c) inference latency on single GPU (ms), (d) amortized diagnostic overhead (%). Compare against: baseline (uniform quantization), EMQ (if available), SNIP (if available), manual per-layer tuning (oracle with layer-wise grid search). Plot Pareto fronts: accuracy vs. model size, accuracy vs. latency. Metric: SCAR should dominate or match baselines while maintaining <5% diagnostic overhead.

Experiments.name: Deterministic Precision Switching Robustness
Experiments.description: Test robustness of block-level routing under distribution shift: (1) train routing on CIFAR-10, evaluate on CIFAR-10.1 (natural distribution shift), (2) compute routing diagnostics on CIFAR-10 training data, apply to CIFAR-100 (different task). Measure accuracy retention and routing stability (do precision assignments change?). Hypothesis: curvature-based routing is more stable than heuristic proxies because it captures intrinsic layer properties. Metric: Accuracy retention under distribution shift (target: >95% of in-distribution accuracy). Compare against EMQ routing stability.

Experiments.name: Ablation Study: Diagnostic Components
Experiments.description: Isolate contributions of each spectral diagnostic: (1) condition number alone (SCAR-κ), (2) gradient spectral decay alone (SCAR-decay), (3) feature entanglement alone (SCAR-entangle), (4) PAC-Bayes bound calibration (SCAR-pb), (5) full SCAR. For ResNet-50 at 5.5 avg bits, report accuracy for each variant. Hypothesis: PAC-Bayes calibration and condition number are most predictive; others provide marginal gains. Metric: Accuracy drop relative to full SCAR (target: no variant exceeds 0.5% drop).

Experiments.name: Computational Overhead Analysis
Experiments.description: Profile diagnostic overhead on single GPU (NVIDIA A100 or V100): (1) power iteration (20 iterations) for condition number: measure time, (2) randomized SVD for spectral decay: measure time, (3) covariance estimation (256 mini-batch samples): measure time, (4) routing decision logic: measure time. Report total diagnostic time as fraction of inference time for different batch sizes (1, 32, 128). Hypothesis: total overhead <5% of inference time. Metrics: (i) absolute diagnostic time (seconds), (ii) overhead ratio (%), (iii) memory overhead for storing diagnostics and routing tables (MB).

Experiments.name: Multi-Seed Variance Quantification
Experiments.description: Run all experiments with 5 random seeds (different mini-batch orderings for diagnostic estimation, different quantization noise initializations). Report mean accuracy and 95% confidence intervals. Hypothesis: SCAR routing is robust to randomness in diagnostic estimation. Metric: Confidence interval width <0.5% of mean accuracy.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral estimation via power iteration may converge slowly for ill-conditioned matrices; mitigated by using randomized SVD and capping iterations at 20 (empirically sufficient for diagnostic purposes).
PAC-Bayes bounds are often loose in practice; we address this by calibrating thresholds empirically rather than using raw bound values, reducing reliance on bound tightness.

Coarse-grained block-level routing (layer or multi-head clusters) may sacrifice fine-grained optimization; ablations will quantify accuracy gaps relative to per-element routing, though deterministic routing is essential for deployment feasibility.
Diagnostic overhead could exceed 5% on very small models or inference-only scenarios; we explicitly profile this and report absolute times, enabling practitioners to assess feasibility.
Generalization across architectures (CNNs, ViTs, LLMs) may vary; we validate on three distinct families, but some architecture-specific tuning of thresholds may be necessary.

Correlation between spectral diagnostics and quantization accuracy may be weaker for highly non-linear quantization schemes (e.g., learned quantization); we focus on uniform and symmetric quantization, the most common deployment scenario.
Reproducibility depends on exact spectral estimation hyperparameters (power iteration count, SVD rank, mini-batch size); we will provide exact algorithmic specifications and version-controlled code.
PAC-Bayes bound derivation assumes specific loss structures; bounds may not hold for all loss functions, limiting theoretical guarantees to standard cross-entropy and language modeling losses.
